mapping.a_element: pre-calculated fragment configurations and interactions (fragment libraries)
mapping.b_element: pre-computed histograms or ensembles from generalized-ensemble simulations (e.g., single/multiple-histogram reweighting)

mapping.a_element: growing a molecule by combining fragments to estimate absolute free energy
mapping.b_element: performing a random walk in potential energy space to sample configurations across temperatures

mapping.a_element: equilibrium ensembles generated at no additional computational cost for validation
mapping.b_element: canonical-ensemble averages obtained from a single generalized-ensemble simulation run

brief_justification: While both methods aim to improve computational efficiency in sampling molecular configurations, Paper A relies on a combinatorial fragment-based approach to estimate absolute free energies, whereas Paper B uses generalized-ensemble algorithms (e.g., multicanonical, replica-exchange) to overcome quasi-ergodicity via energy-space random walks. The core mechanisms—fragment assembly vs. ensemble reweighting—are fundamentally different.
